# Questions in Livestream 147

Live Stream #147: Celebrating 12,000 Subs... We Hope.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=452" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:32</a> | If God exists outside time, how would he experience time before creation? | He says he would not; time belongs to creation, while God stands outside it and acts as cause rather than as a time-bound effect. | He answers that God would not experience time before creation because there is no time outside the created order. He connects time with causation, says God is cause rather than effect, and describes God as able to act in time by inserting himself into creation while remaining outside the space-time continuum. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=695" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:35</a> | Could you speak on the books of Jasher, especially their reliability and concerns? | He says the modern books of Jasher should not be treated as the ancient text referenced in Scripture. | He distinguishes the lost biblical Book of Jasher, cited for example in Joshua 10:13, from later works circulated under that name. The text people can read today is treated as pseudo-Jasher, a medieval forgery known to scholars as such, so it should not be used as if it preserves the ancient source. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=815" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:35</a> | Does making icons and praying to saints violate Deuteronomy's command against necromancy? | He says making icons is not the same as necromancy, but praying to saints raises concerns about certainty and efficacy. | He reads Deuteronomy 18 as forbidding divination by contacting the dead, then contrasts that with redeemed saints being alive in Christ. He still warns that icons may misidentify who is redeemed and that praying to saints can imply a questionable theology of using saints to influence God, so he regards the practice as concerning and not very efficacious rather than straightforward necromancy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=1271" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:11</a> | How do we identify temple dedication literature? | He says dedication texts have recognizable ritual, royal, and temple-context markers, but genre identification still depends on evidence. | He gives three main markers: the text must concern the dedication or building of a temple, it is normally tied to a notable epoch or event in the distant past, and it often uses a numerological span of years such as 400, 480, or 720 between that epoch and the new building. He adds that the corpus is small and many examples are short inscriptions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=1441" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:01</a> | Will there be more Revelation Symbolism Made Simple videos? | He says yes, it is on the to-do list, but work and health delays have slowed the series. | He says a script is already in progress and he wants the series to be careful, strong, and methodical. Work schedule changes and recent illness set back more complex videos, but he says they still want to do more in the series. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=1556" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:56</a> | What is the value of comparing the Bible with ancient Near Eastern literature like Enuma Elish, Gilgamesh, and Atrahasis? | He says comparison helps readers see shared cultural background and distinct biblical claims more clearly. | He says comparison anchors the Bible in history and in the thought world in which it was written, helping ordinary Christians understand why certain material is in the text. He warns against parallel mania based on cosmetic similarities, but says real comparisons can clarify the Bible's aims, such as reading Genesis in terms of sacred space rather than without ancient Near Eastern context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=1694" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:14</a> | How can someone with no ancient Near Eastern languages do comparative study responsibly? | He recommends using translations and good secondary scholarship while recognizing language limitations. | He says much of the needed material is available in translation, naming collections such as Context of Scripture, Pritchard's ancient Near Eastern texts, and translations of Egyptian literature. He also recommends using scholars who connect the evidence, such as John Walton, so readers without the languages can still study responsibly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=1826" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:26</a> | Do Old Testament passages about Amalek justify Israel's actions in Gaza or calls for total war? | He says they should not be used as the justification, though there is a limited comparison between Amalek and Hamas attacking civilians. | He says the Amalek passages are not needed to justify Israel's defensive action; common principles of protecting civilians and borders are enough. He allows a comparison in tactics because Amalek and Hamas are described as targeting the vulnerable and noncombatants, but he does not treat that comparison as a biblical warrant for total war. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=2060" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:20</a> | When will the feeling of not being good, holy, or knowledgeable enough before Christ go away? | He says assurance rests in Christ and grace, not in reaching a self-measured threshold of holiness or knowledge. | He says the feeling is not wrong and may never go away, because Christian maturity brings a growing dissatisfaction with one's own spiritual progress. He contrasts that with self-confidence, says no one becomes good or holy enough by personal effort, and points the listener back to Christ's completed work and righteousness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=2435" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:35</a> | In John 5:1-9, was there an actual angel who stirred the waters? | He treats the stirring-water wording as textually and interpretively complicated rather than a simple historical claim. | He says the word can refer either to a spiritual being or to a messenger, and in this context the bubbling water itself could be the message to enter the pool. Because the spiritual-level details are unclear, he leaves the answer open: it could have been an angel, or it could have been a sign conveyed through the stirring water. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=2590" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:10</a> | How would you translate the wording about Ittai in 2 Samuel 15:21? | He says context matters and discusses the wording in relation to loyalty and oath language. | He treats Ittai primarily as a name and says its meaning is uncertain. "My companion" is a possible and acceptable translation, and "is with me" is another possibility, but he rejects "Yahweh is with me" as a proper translation unless the divine element is bracketed because it is not clearly part of the name. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=2768" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:08</a> | In Genesis 39, should Joseph be translated as "he increases" or "Yahweh increases"? | He says it is "he increases." | He says Joseph is an imperfective name but not a theophoric one. Because the name lacks the Yahwistic element that would identify Yahweh as the subject, he translates it simply as "he increases." |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=2832" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:12</a> | Were early Israelites pantheistic? | He says no; Israelite religion must be discussed carefully, but pantheism is not the right category. | He says early Israelites were not specifically pantheistic, though there may have been limited pantheistic influence from contact with the Aten cult during the Amarna period. He distinguishes pantheism from henotheism and says Sinai covenant theology and transcendence would collapse those elements into monotheism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=3011" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:11</a> | If a nearby language certificate only offers three semesters of each language, what do you advise? | He recommends a full MDiv or comparable program with formal language training rather than a certificate or self-study path. | He says certificate programs are not real degrees and recommends an institutional program that requires the languages. He cautions that self-study can work only after someone already has a language framework, because early Hebrew, advanced Greek, and unfamiliar ancient languages need classroom discipline and correction to avoid propagating mistakes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=3383" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:23</a> | Can you host a poll to see who would help raise $50,000 for a 24-hour live stream? | He says he already has one GoFundMe and would leave that fundraising to others. | He says he is not likely to start another fundraising campaign while one is already running. If people want the 24-hour stream badly enough, he says they would need to organize the fundraising themselves. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=3448" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:28</a> | Any updates on the Egypt trip, brochure, payment information, and deadlines? | He says the brochure and trip information are coming and gives practical status updates. | He says he had approved the brochure proofs and expected the brochure shortly. He describes the trip as still moving forward, notes that people had already expressed interest, and says the coming brochure would provide practical participation details. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=3584" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:44</a> | Did ancient pagans sing hymns? | He says yes, pagans sang hymns and had religious songs. | He says pagans did sing religious songs, including Egyptian hymns such as the Hymn to the Aten, though not many examples survive. He mentions instruments such as timbrel, sistrum, lyre, and drums, and says hymns could be adapted across religious traditions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=4231" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:31</a> | In Luke's "sell your cloak and buy a sword" saying, does `machairon` mean a tool rather than a combat sword? | He says no; a `machairon` is a short defensive sword, not a tool. | He argues the context does not make sense if Jesus means a generic tool, since selling a cloak to buy one would be strange. He treats `machairon` as a short sidearm or defensive weapon, something closer to a short sword, and says the tool-only reading looks driven by ideology rather than lexical or contextual evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=4639" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:19</a> | Did the Israelites cause the Bronze Age collapse? | He says no, but they benefited from the collapse. | He says the collapse was already underway before Israel's settlement, with factors such as Hittite famine, Sea Peoples pressure, internal problems, and plague. Israelite occupation of the promised land may be a symptom of that broader collapse, like Philistine settlement on the coast, but not its cause. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=4761" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:21</a> | Where is the first alphabet from? | He points to the earliest currently known alphabetic text from Syria, around 2300 BC, while noting it is still undeciphered. | He identifies the earliest known alphabetic text as coming from Syria, from an Early Bronze Age tomb at Umm el-Marra around 2300 BC. He says it is undeciphered but alphabetic because of the limited sign set and repeated symbols, unlike hieroglyphic systems with many more distinct signs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=4925" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:05</a> | What are your thoughts on Benjamin Sommer's Many Faces of God? | He says he has not read it and will not comment on or recommend a book he has not read. | He says he has heard a few comments about the book and might look it up or read it, but he refuses to give an evaluation without having read it himself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=4963" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:43</a> | What is the biblical understanding of free will, faith, and predestination? | He says the question is too broad for a livestream answer and needs narrower terms. | He explains that each term carries major debates: free will can involve Calvinism, Arminianism, Molinism, determinism, or compatibilism; faith can mean intellectual assent, trust, and fidelity; predestination raises election and selection questions. Because the combined topic is enormous and more suited to a theologian, he advises asking a more focused question. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=5165" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:05</a> | How did the Philistines attack Israel from the swamp? | He explains it from the geography of the Philistine coastal plain and marshy areas. | He says the Philistines were already established on the western side of the Shephelah valley, with Israelite cities on the eastern hills. Because the conflict zone lay between them, the Philistines did not have to cross the swamp to attack; they were already positioned beyond it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=5411" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:11</a> | Who were the Semites who remained in Egypt from 1830 until its abandonment in the Nineteenth Dynasty? | He identifies them in relation to Semitic communities in Egypt and cautions against over-specific identification. | He says Semitic groups had been entering the Nile Delta since around 2100 BC as the Neolithic wet period ended and the Sahara and Sinai dried out. He identifies these earlier migrants broadly as Libyans, Amorites, Canaanites, and other Levantine peoples, with the Israelites as a later wave rather than the only Semitic presence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=5646" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:06</a> | Since lower-class Egyptians could not afford fancy funerary goods, was embalming expected for them? | He says elite funerary practice should not be projected onto everyone in Egypt. | He says lower-class Egyptians tried to buy the best afterlife they could afford, even if that meant only a few shabti figures or selected Book of the Dead spells instead of full elite equipment. They were not assumed to be automatically lost, but they were seen as disadvantaged by lacking the full set of funerary resources. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=5963" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:23</a> | Which games did your PhD ruin for you? | He says archaeology-heavy games, especially ones like Tomb Raider and Assassin's Creed, are hardest for him to ignore critically. | He says games with Egyptian or archaeological themes feel too much like work, so Tomb Raider and Assassin's Creed Origins were ruined for him. He had not played Uncharted, was not broadly an Assassin's Creed player, and instead preferred games such as Dishonored, Far Cry, Starfield, Fallout, and Skyrim. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=6231" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:51</a> | What is the best single piece of evidence for the Exodus? | He points first to the Ark of the Covenant, but stresses that Exodus evidence is cumulative rather than a one-proof argument. | He says the Ark's Egyptian-style furniture points to a plausible late New Kingdom range, and he also names the Merneptah victory stele, Avaris abandonment, bread bowls, and Egyptian vocabulary. He frames the argument as inductive: ancient history usually works by the preponderance of multiple pieces of evidence, not by one decisive artifact. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=6369" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:09</a> | What Egyptian vocabulary appears in the book of Exodus? | He discusses Egyptian loanwords and names in Exodus, while noting that vocabulary arguments need care. | He says Exodus vocabulary is not limited to mineral terms and includes Egyptian-linked words for linen, river reeds, the Sea of Reeds, and place names. He argues the mineral evidence points more naturally to Sinai and eastern Egypt than to Saudi Arabia, and he treats the vocabulary as part of a broader cumulative argument. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=6747" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:52:27</a> | Can you give a refresher on the history of young-earth and old-earth creationism? | He sketches the modern history and says the categories are not as old or simple as many assume. | He says it is not true that old-earth creationism only exists to accommodate evolution, since older-earth readings predate Darwin and were debated among Christians before the modern evolution debate. He describes young-earth creationism as a more codified modern movement associated especially with Seventh-day Adventist influence, while also saying earlier Christians held a range of creation views. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=7304" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:44</a> | How is your new book coming? | He says multiple projects are underway at once, including editing one draft and writing or proposing several others. | He compares his work style to handling several projects simultaneously: a rough draft being edited, another book being written, a book proposal, a video game, and a second proposal, aside from channel scripts and videos. He also connects fiction work to cultural engagement, saying it can reach audiences in ways nonfiction or YouTube may not. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=7791" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:51</a> | Do people even read nowadays? | He says yes, people still read, though genres and formats shift. | He says publishers have predicted their own demise for decades, but fiction sales remain strong and genres rise and fall. He points to Kindle as reviving fiction reading for many people while noting he still prefers tangible books, and he distinguishes dead or declining genres from reading as a whole. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=8230" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:10</a> | Are there resources on ancient Egyptian martial arts before Greek influence? | He says there are not many good resources and the evidence is limited. | He says Egyptian martial practice existed but has often been interpreted through a Greek wrestling lens, so the field lacks good work. He describes the Egyptian material as closer in some ways to jiu-jitsu than Greek wrestling, used for entertainment and physical training, with evidence especially around places such as Beni Hasan. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=8415" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:15</a> | Can antinatalists be shown sense by comparison with doomsday claims? | He compares antinatalist and doomsday arguments to recurring predictions of collapse that keep moving forward. | After defining antinatalism, he distinguishes a personal choice not to have children from claiming it is morally wrong for others to have children. He says the stronger public argument is fear-driven and compares it to repeated doomsday predictions that keep setting collapse a few years away, making debate unlikely to be useful. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=8846" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:26</a> | Did ancient Egyptians have anything comparable to judicial combat or gladiators? | He says no; Egyptian legal and punishment practices were not judicial combat. | He says Egypt had neither judicial combat nor gladiators in the Roman sense. They did have wrestling and they did use beatings in interrogation, often before questioning, but that was not a legal duel to decide a case or entertainment built around killing combatants. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=9044" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:44</a> | How do you develop and execute a thesis on a topic that is not well written about? | He says to start from evidence, define the question clearly, and build a defensible argument from the available material. | He says research often starts with noticing something odd, underexplained, or interesting in the texts, then following more evidence until a fuller explanation emerges. He now favors focused, incremental research questions because paradigm-changing work is harder to publish, harder to defend, and often meets strong resistance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=9350" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:50</a> | Must all academic papers be published with someone who has a PhD? | He says no, but credentials and collaboration can affect access, review, and reception. | He says a PhD is not strictly required for academic publication, although some journals may require one and most peer-reviewed venues demand high quality. It is possible to publish as an independent researcher, and he notes that his own first peer-reviewed paper was published when he only had a bachelor's degree, but doing so is more difficult. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=9702" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:42</a> | Besides Abrahamic and Egyptian religion, which religion is your favorite? | He gives a personal preference answer rather than a scholarly ranking. | He says vampire religions remain especially interesting to him because they blend elements from new religious movements, Egyptian symbolism, gnosticism, Lilith traditions, demon and angel myths, and distinctive ethical systems. He emphasizes that this was a rapidly changing movement and that being among the first to study it was academically interesting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=9993" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:33</a> | Because Israel exists now, are we necessarily going to have the Second Coming within 500 or 1,000 years? | He says no one knows; it could be soon or far off, and Christians are not meant to know the date. | He affirms the historic Christian expectation of a Second Coming and says the existence of Israel is consistent with that expectation, but it does not yield a timetable. His practical answer is that believers should remain faithful to the end whether the end is in five years, five hundred years, or much later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=10132" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:48:52</a> | Why does David still affirm the deity and resurrection of Christ? | He says the historical and theological arguments remain strong, and atheist objections are not new. | He says he believes Christ's deity and messiahship because that is what Scripture and the Gospels teach, and because Christ appears in the Old Testament as the church fathers argued. He adds that critics are not saying anything new; the same objections have been addressed for centuries. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=10574" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:14</a> | Does Akkad's limited historical span narrow down Nimrod's identity? | He says maybe, but he has not done the prerequisite work to answer confidently. | He says he would need to look more closely at Akkad and the archaeology before deciding whether the timing narrows Nimrod to a specific candidate such as Ninurta. Since he has not worked through the Nimrod question, he leaves it open rather than giving a firm answer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=10647" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:57:27</a> | How does one rest in Christ? | He says to accept Christ's grace and unmerited favor. | He says resting in Christ means accepting grace and recognizing that Christ has done the heavy lifting. He appeals to "it is finished" as paid in full, says good works do not save, and frames Christian obedience as responding to Christ's completed work rather than trying to earn heaven. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=10794" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:59:54</a> | What geological evidence exists for a global flood? | He says he is not a geologist and does not present himself as able to adjudicate that evidence deeply. | He says some young-earth creationists point to marine fossils on mountains or in deserts, while others explain those by tectonic shifts. He does not take a side, saying geology is not his field and that the Noah narrative's theological point concerns sacred humanity and repopulation more than adjudicating global versus local flood evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=10980" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:00</a> | What is the best argument for the truthfulness of Christianity? | He points toward the resurrection and the broader cumulative case rather than a single simplistic proof. | He says he is an educator rather than an apologist, but if asked about the truthfulness of Christianity he goes to the resurrection. In his view, the faith stands or falls on the resurrection of Jesus Christ, and he thinks the evidence is good while recommending New Testament apologetics specialists for fuller treatment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=11152" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:52</a> | What are your thoughts on H. P. Lovecraft? | He discusses Lovecraft's style and influence while noting problems in the author's worldview and character. | He says Lovecraft was a great writer whose strength was atmosphere, suspense, and the feeling created by words rather than merely the monsters or mythos. He had read Lovecraft's fiction and writing advice but says he cannot comment on Lovecraft's philosophy because he does not know it well. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=11473" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:11:13</a> | How do I get an MDiv at International Trinity College, and what are your thoughts on the University of Arizona? | He says to evaluate programs carefully, especially accreditation, rigor, and what the credential will be used for. | He clarifies that he went to Trinity Evangelical Divinity School, part of Trinity International University, and says an MDiv there requires applying to the divinity school with a bachelor's degree. He praises TEDS master's programs, is more cautious about its undergrad and PhD programs, and says the University of Arizona is a strong school while warning against confusing it with for-profit institutions such as University of Phoenix. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=11868" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:17:48</a> | Do Moses, Enoch, and Elijah's ascensions prove the rapture will happen? | He says no; Moses did not ascend, and Enoch and Elijah do not provide a necessary connection to rapture theology. | He argues the case fails because Moses died, Enoch and Elijah's ascensions are not punishments, and the relevant rapture texts are about summary judgment in historic Christian interpretation. He says the dispensational pre-tribulational version of the rapture is a later development and that the argument lacks the necessary connection needed to prove the claim. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=12238" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:58</a> | What was the purpose of the Tree of Life in Eden, and could it have been uneaten before the fall? | He discusses the Tree of Life as tied to life and access, while not overclaiming beyond the text. | He says the Tree of Life appears available before the fall, with no prohibition against eating it, but after the fall continued access would have meant living forever in a sinful state. He frames Adam and Eve as having contingent immortality that, if obedience continued, could have become unconditional through eating from the Tree of Life. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=12454" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:27:34</a> | Should Christians be afraid of atheist polemics? | He says no; the objections are not new, and believers should not treat atheist criticism as unprecedented or automatically devastating. | He says Christians need not fear atheist polemics because the objections have been raised and answered before, even if a listener has not personally heard the answers. He suggests engaging arguments in moderation, checking whether claims are true and how others have responded, while recognizing that old objections are often repackaged. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=12728" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:08</a> | If biblical ages are accurate, was Terah about the same age as Abraham when he had Abraham? | He says the biblical ages should likely be read through abbreviated genealogies and clan identity rather than as simple individual lifespans. | He notes that a flat calculation can make Terah about 130 when Abraham is born, older than Abraham when Isaac is born, which complicates the claim that Abraham and Sarah's old-age conception is unique on a strictly literal reading. He argues that abbreviated genealogies, missing intermediaries, and clan longevity better explain the data than treating every age as a direct one-person chronology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=13204" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:40:04</a> | What historical evidence is there for Abraham's father? | He discusses the limits of direct evidence and how names, places, and cultural context can be evaluated. | He points to the Mari letters published by Jack Sasson, especially references to a Terah/Teru figure associated with Urkesh. He says the time, place, name, and biographical details are strikingly similar to Abraham's father, though he does not expect much new evidence because no new Mari letters are currently forthcoming. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=13433" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:43:53</a> | Does belief in an early Exodus date usually go hand in glove with a literal reading of Genesis ages, and should one just smile and nod? | He says the views often cluster, especially in American biblical chronology, and are hard to dislodge. | He says early-date Exodus, young-earth creationism, and literal Genesis 5 and 11 chronology often overlap in American churches and organizations because people have been enculturated into that framework. He still says young-earth views can be defended with rigor and nuance, but American biblical chronology creates difficulties and superficial arguments do unnecessary harm. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=13843" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:43</a> | Why does God love us despite who we are? | He says it is part of the mystery of God's love and grace, not something humans earn. | He says God would have been just to judge humanity, wipe it out, and start over, so the fact that he loves the unworthy reveals the character of God rather than human merit. The answer is not that humans give God a reason, but that God is merciful, gracious, and loving. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=13972" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:52</a> | Does Ezekiel 20:25-26 support the claim that orthodox Yahwism included human sacrifice? | He says no; in context the passage condemns human sacrifice rather than promoting it. | He reads from earlier in Ezekiel 20 to show Israel's rebellion, idolatry, profaned Sabbaths, and unacceptable gifts. In that context, God giving them statutes that were not good and making them desolate is judgment language, not a command, so he says using verses 25-26 for orthodox Yahwistic human sacrifice is quote mining. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/mVea6OvoRic?t=14551" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:02:31</a> | Which church fathers leaned toward an older-earth reading? | He says he recalls Irenaeus, Hippolytus, and Augustine in that discussion, while also saying the references should be checked. | He gives a cautious memory-based answer rather than a firm citation list, saying Irenaeus, Hippolytus, and Augustine come to mind, but he is not sure which specific father held which view. He recommends looking up sources on early church fathers and origins rather than treating his recall as definitive. |
